preamble.summary: Daniel 1 in the LXX/Theodotion is relatively close to the MT. Theodotion's version, which became the church standard, follows the MT closely. The Old Greek (OG) has minor differences in phrasing but the narrative structure is preserved.
preamble.notable_variants: The OG has slight differences in the naming of the chief eunuch and in the description of the youths' diet. Theodotion aligns closely with the MT throughout.
preamble.structural_notes: Both Greek versions preserve the 21-verse structure of the MT.

variant_notes: No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.
Both OG and Theodotion render Adonai as kyrios. No meaningful difference.
Greek uses a compound word archieunouchos rather than a construct phrase. Same meaning.
Theodotion uses paidaria (young boys); the OG uses neaniskous (young men). The MT yěladim can mean either.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.
Greek transliterations of the Babylonian names differ slightly from Hebrew forms. Theodotion: Daniel = Baltasar; OG similar.

verses.lxx_reading: καὶ ἔθετο Δανιηλ ἐπὶ τὴν καρδίαν αὐτοῦ
verses.variant_rendering: And Daniel set it upon his heart
verses.mt_rendering: And Daniel purposed in his heart
variant_notes: Literal Greek calque of the Hebrew idiom. Both mean 'resolved/determined.'
LXX renders chesed as eleos (mercy), a standard equivalence throughout the Septuagint.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.
OG treats ham-meltsar as a proper name (Amelsad). Theodotion has Αμελσαρ. The MT likely intends a title ('the steward/guardian'), not a name.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.
OG adds that Daniel was also given understanding in 'every word and vision' — a slight expansion over the MT and Theodotion.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.
No significant variant between the LXX/Theodotion and the MT for this verse.
Greek uses a multiplicative adverb rather than the Hebrew idiom 'ten hands.'

verses.variant_rendering: until the first year of Cyrus
verses.mt_rendering: until the first year of Cyrus
variant_notes: Both traditions agree on this chronological note. OG phrasing is slightly different but the meaning is identical.
